preFix.title: Spinning Soay wool
preFix.subtitle: Working with the short, kemp-heavy fleece of Britain's most ancient sheep breed
preFix.excerpt: Soay fleece is a two-coat primitive fibre: fine undercoat and coarse kemp. Separate the layers by hand, spin the undercoat on a supported spindle or wheel, and blend the kemp into textured batts.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from breed-specific spinning references including Deborah Robson and Carol Ekarius 'The Fleece and Fiber Sourcebook', supported by general primitive-breed spinning practice.

content.text: Soay sheep are the oldest surviving domestic breed in Britain, kept on the island of Soay in the St Kilda archipelago for thousands of years. Their fleece is unlike any modern breed: a two-coat structure in which a fine, soft undercoat sits beneath a coarser outer layer loaded with
kemp
. The fleece is rooed (shed naturally) rather than shorn, so locks arrive with a tapered tip and a naturally clean break at the root.
Staple length
runs 3 to 7 cm, which is short by most standards and demands a high
twist ratio
to keep the single coherent. Colour ranges from dark chocolate brown through fawn to a pale honey; because kemp does not accept dye, the natural palette is the yarn's palette.

attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin. Tie hair back and roll sleeves before sitting at the wheel.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

items.name: Basin of warm water (40 °C) and dish soap

content.text: Method
Step 1: Separate the two coats

Hold a lock at the base and gently pull the fine undercoat away from the coarser outer layer with the other hand. The two coats separate with light tension because the fibre types differ in diameter and scale structure. Set the undercoat in one pile and the kemp-heavy outer fibres in another. Work through the whole fleece before spinning.
Step 2: Prepare the undercoat on a supported spindle
Rest a
supported spindle

in a smooth bowl. Take a small pinch of the undercoat, pre-draft it slightly by pulling between both hands until it narrows to roughly 1 cm across, then introduce it to the leader yarn. The short staple means the twist travels fast: keep the drafting hand close to the twist-front and use a modified short draw, advancing only 2 to 3 cm at a time before allowing twist to enter the draft zone.
Step 3: Spinning on the wheel

If using a Saxony wheel, set a high drive-band tension and a fast whorl ratio. Soay undercoat needs more twist per centimetre than commercial merino top, so a whorl that gives 10 to 14 twists per 30 cm of treadle stroke is a useful starting point. Observe the angle of the twist helix in the single: for a yarn that will hold together in a knit fabric, the helix should sit at around 30 to 40 degrees from the vertical.
Step 4: Blend the kemp layer

The coarser outer layer is too rough for next-to-skin wear but works well blended. Load the drum carder with 60% natural Soay outer fibre and 40% a coarser wool such as Shetland or Jacob. Card until the batt is uniform. The resulting
rolags
can be spun with a woollen draw into a textured rug or weaving yarn. Kemp will remain visible as bright white flecks, which is characteristic of the fabric.
Step 5: Wind off onto the niddy-noddy

Once the bobbin or cop is full, wind the single onto the niddy-noddy using figure-8 circuits around both crossbars. Count wraps to calculate yardage. Tie the skein in four places with cotton waste yarn, then soak in 40 °C water for 20 minutes to set the twist. Squeeze without wringing, roll in a towel, and hang with a small weight to dry straight.

items.fix: Move to a faster whorl or spin the supported spindle more briskly. Pre-draft less aggressively so more fibres overlap at each draft point.
items.cause: Twist ratio too low for the short staple length

items.fix: Shorten the draft to no more than the staple length (3 to 5 cm). Use a short forward draw rather than long draw.
items.cause: Draft zone too long relative to staple length
items.problem: Lumpy, uneven singles with thick and thin sections

items.fix: Accept it as a feature of the yarn, or restrict spinning to the separated undercoat only and use the kemp layer for blended batt projects.
items.cause: Normal characteristic of the breed; kemp is not anchored by scale structure
items.problem: Kemp fibres work out of the finished single

items.fix: Card or comb more thoroughly to remove kemp before spinning, or switch entirely to the fine undercoat fraction.
items.cause: Kemp fibres have reached the yarn surface
